feedingProfile.preferredNpk: compost / low-nitrogen; little to no feeding needed

commonIssues: Root rot in wet or poorly drained soil; thyme dislikes excess moisture far more than cold (Missouri Botanical Garden; RHS).

Becomes overly woody and leggy after several years; cut back in early spring and summer, leaving about five inches of growth, and replace plants periodically (NC State Extension; Old Farmer's Almanac).

Container minimum (2 L) reflects RHS guidance of a pot at least 15 cm wide and 10 cm deep for one plant; thyme is shallow-rooted, so wide-but-shallow pots with excellent drainage suit it better than deep ones.

source.citation: RHS - How to grow Thyme (Grow Your Own): full sun and free-draining conditions; container at least 15 cm (6 in) wide and 10 cm (4 in) deep for one plant; peat-free soil-based compost with up to 25% horticultural grit; water deeply only when soil is completely dry; most widely available varieties withstand UK frosts; damp is a greater risk than cold.
RHS - How to grow Thyme: watering guidance (water deeply only when soil completely dry; drought-tolerant, dislikes excess moisture / root rot).

Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder - Thymus vulgaris: USDA hardiness zones 5 to 9; height 0.50-1.00 ft; full sun; dry to medium, well-drained soil; drought tolerant; bloom May to July; low maintenance; susceptible to root rot if soil too moist; becomes overly woody after several years.
Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder - Thymus vulgaris: prefers average, dry to medium, well-drained, sandy/rocky soils; dislikes wet conditions (root rot).

NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox - Thymus vulgaris: USDA zones 5a-9b; full sun (6 or more hours of direct sun a day); drought-tolerant and frost-tolerant; low maintenance, trim when leggy, leave five inches of growth; neutral to alkaline pH; intolerant of wet soils and shade.
NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox - Thymus vulgaris: frost-tolerant, hardy to zone 5 (lower limit ~ -29 C / -20 F per USDA zone 5 minimum); intolerant of wet soils.

The Old Farmer's Almanac - Thyme: full sun, 6-8 hours of bright light; drought-friendly with low watering needs, let soil dry out between waterings; herbs like thyme, lavender and rosemary should not need feeding and do best in nutrient-poor, drier soils; if fertilizing, use compost in early spring; trim when leggy; cut back early spring and summer.
The Old Farmer's Almanac - Thyme: light/sun-hour guidance (6-8 hours bright light) and drought-friendly watering behaviour.

Confidence note: baseIntervalDays (watering ~7) and hotWeatherMultiplier (0.5) are conservative estimates. Authoritative sources (RHS, Almanac, NC State, MBG) prescribe watering by soil-dryness rather than a fixed interval ('water deeply only when soil is completely dry'); thyme is strongly drought-tolerant, so the 7-day base and reduced hot-weather frequency encode 'let it dry out' rather than a sourced fixed cadence. feedingProfile.baseIntervalDays (60) is likewise an estimate: sources say little-to-no feeding is needed, so a long ~60-day/early-spring cadence is used to approximate 'rarely feed'. heatStressC (38) is an estimate; sources emphasize cold-hardiness and drought tolerance but do not state a numeric heat-stress threshold.
